| Horned Melon | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Fruits |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients  | Fruit

The **horned melon**, also known as the **kiwano melon**, **African horned melon**, and many other trade names,[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) is a close relative of cucumbers and melons.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) It is native to southern and central Africa.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The fruit is oval in shape, a few inches in length with spines all over.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) It has a thick, orange-yellow rind with a pale yellow-green pulp and many crunchy edible seeds inside.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The flavor of the pulp is slightly sweet and a bit tart with a flavor mix reminiscent of bananas, lime and cucumber.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

## Seasonality

[edit | edit source]

The melons are generally available year-round as imports from Africa and New Zealand,[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) though the peak season is around spring and summer.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Outside of regions where they are grown, these melons are usually only available in specialty markets. Select melons with a bright orange color and no bruising or spots.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) It is best to purchase a melon that has the horns intact, as damaged horns may be a sign of rough handling. However, know that the spines can damage adjacent produce.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

Unripe melons may be stored at room temperature for quite a long time, on the order of weeks to months.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) Do not chill it, as this speeds its deterioration.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

There is no way to peel the skin off of the melon, so the fruit needs to be scooped or squeezed out of the melon before using.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The melon may be cut in half or into wedges to help extract the fruit pulp.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The pulp may be eaten by itself, used as a topping for a sweet dessert, added to a fruit or green salad, or processed into beverages, sorbets, and more.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) If desired, the pulp can be pressed through a sieve to remove the seeds.[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) The shell may be used as a serving dish once the pulp is removed,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:3-4) but the rind should not be eaten. In some regions, the whole fruit is baked or roasted.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) Immature melons can be peeled and eaten like cucumbers.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6) The fruit may also be used solely as a garnish.[[6]](#cite_note-:5-6)

## Recipes

[edit | edit source]

Category *Recipes using horned melon* not found
